did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated 'Light-Dermo', a lightweight pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model based on ShuffleNet architecture with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks, for the classification of seven classes of pigmented skin lesions (PSLs) using dermoscopy images from HAM10000, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel lightweight CNN model (Light-Dermo) for classifying pigmented skin lesions. It details the methodology (ShuffleNet architecture with SE blocks), data acquisition (HAM10000, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020 datasets), and reports specific experimental results (accuracy of 99.14%, specificity, sensitivity, F1-score). The structure includes Introduction, Methods, Results, and Conclusion sections typical of primary research articles.

title: Light-Dermo: A Lightweight Pretrained Convolution Neural Network for the Diagnosis of Multiclass Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study developed Light-Dermo, a lightweight pretrained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the ShuffleNet architecture with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks, for the classification of seven classes of pigmented skin lesions (PSLs). The model was trained and evaluated using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets, which were augmented to address class imbalance. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the proposed model's performance against state-of-the-art deep learning models in terms of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and F1-score. The results demonstrated that Light-Dermo achieved an accuracy of 99.14%, a specificity of 98.20%, a sensitivity of 97.45%, and an F1-score of 98.1%. The study concludes that Light-Dermo offers a computationally efficient and accurate solution for multiclass skin lesion diagnosis, suitable for real-time applications on resource-constrained devices.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of pigmented skin lesions (PSLs): actinic keratoses (AK), basal cell carcinoma (BCC), benign keratosis (BK), dermatofibroma (DF), melanoma (MEL), melanocytic nevi (NV), and vascular skin lesions (VASC).
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of pigmented skin lesions using dermoscopy images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for seven lesion classes. Performance metrics reported include accuracy (99.14%), specificity (98.20%), sensitivity (97.45%), and F1-score (98.1%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The 'Human Against Machine with 10,000 Training Images' dataset, a public dermatoscopy image collection made available by the Harvard database to provide training data for automating skin cancer lesion classification.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training the Light-Dermo model after data augmentation.
use.other: Combined with ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 to form the initial 14,000 image dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISIC challenge containing nine classes of pigmented skin lesions, accessed via the Kaggle platform. The study selected seven specific classes from this dataset.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training the Light-Dermo model after data augmentation.
use.other: Combined with HAM10000 and ISIC-2020 to form the initial 14,000 image dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the ISIC challenge emphasizing multi-class predictions. The study selected a subset of images from this dataset.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training the Light-Dermo model after data augmentation.
use.other: Combined with HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 to form the initial 14,000 image dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate True Positive (TP), True Negative (TN), False Positive (FP), and False Negative (FN) values for evaluating classification performance.
To calculate the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient between actual and expected values, providing a balanced measure unaffected by class imbalance.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the Light-Dermo model against state-of-the-art deep learning models.
To report the classification results (ACC, SP, SE, PR, F1-Score, MCC) for each of the seven PSL classes, indicating variability in performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) of classification metrics such as accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient.

Combined Dataset (HAM10000, ISIC-2019, I.description: Results for the proposed Light-Dermo model (ShuffleNet-Light) and comparative models on the combined dataset of 28,000 augmented images (14,000 original).
Combined Dataset (HAM10000, ISIC-2019, I.notes: The Light-Dermo model outperformed state-of-the-art deep learning models in many cases. The original ShuffleNet performed poorly in 40 iterations. MobileNet achieved the highest accuracy among the comparative models (90.8%). Results are based on 40 epochs.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using weights from Inception-v3 pretrained on the ImageNet dataset.

modifications: Incorporation of Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks as channelwise attention (CA) mechanisms.
Use of depthwise and pointwise separable CNNs (DPS-CNN).
Integration of GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit) as a nonlinear activation function to reduce overfitting.
architecture_details.structure: Four stages (Stage 1 to Stage 4) derived from ShuffleNet, modified formation compared to original ShuffleNet.

training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7-3770 CPU, 16 GB RAM, Nvidia GTX 1080 GPU

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected (FC) layer followed by Softmax
classification_or_output_head.output_neurons: 7 (encoding seven classes of pigmented skin lesions)
classification_or_output_head.original_head_replacement: The original final layer was replaced with an output layer equal to the number of classes.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation to balance class distribution and increase dataset size from 14,000 to 28,000 images
Image resizing to a uniform size of 256 × 256 × 3
Adjustment of brightness and contrast (brightness range [0.9, 1.1])
Region-of-interest (ROI) selection from the centroid of pigmented skin lesions

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Addition of Gaussian noise with a noise value of 0.45.
Data augmentation was applied to balance class distribution, expanding the dataset from 14,000 to 28,000 images, resulting in 4,000 images per class for all seven classes.

conclusions: The proposed Light-Dermo model, a lightweight CNN based on ShuffleNet with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks, achieved an accuracy of 99.14%, specificity of 98.20%, sensitivity of 97.45%, and F1-score of 98.1% on a combined dataset of 28,000 augmented images from HAM10000, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020.

Light-Dermo outperformed state-of-the-art deep learning models (including AlexNet, MobileNet, ResNet18, Inception-v3, Xception, and original ShuffleNet) in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency.

The model is computationally efficient, requiring only 1.9 million parameters and 67.3 million FLOPs, with a model size of 9.3 MB and a GPU inference speed of 0.6 ms, making it suitable for resource-constrained devices.
Data augmentation was critical for performance, as the model achieved its highest classification accuracy when combined with data augmentation techniques to address class imbalance.
The integration of channelwise attention (SE blocks) and GELU activation functions helped enhance precision and reduce overfitting compared to the baseline ShuffleNet architecture.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding high interclass similarity and intraclass variability across some pigmented skin lesion classes, which can make achieving high classification capacity for every class challenging.
Light-Dermo is designed to assist dermatologists in the better diagnosis of multiclass pigmented skin lesions and is suitable for real-time applications on embedded, IoT, and mobile devices.
important_remarks: The study combines three public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020) but does not specify the exact number of images contributed by ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020, only that the total initial dataset was 14,000 images.

Data augmentation was used to balance class distribution, expanding the dataset from 14,000 to 28,000 images with 4,000 images per class; this synthetic balancing may not reflect real-world class prevalence.
The paper acknowledges limitations regarding high interclass similarity and intraclass variability among pigmented skin lesions, which can challenge classification capacity.
The model was trained and evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation on a combined dataset, but external validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets is not reported.

The study claims suitability for resource-constrained devices (embedded, IoT, mobile) based on low parameter count (1.9M) and FLOPs (67.3M), but does not report performance on actual mobile hardware or latency in real-world clinical workflows.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values) to support the significance of performance differences between Light-Dermo and comparative models.

The code is made publicly available on GitHub, which supports reproducibility, but the specific version or commit hash used for the reported results is not explicitly detailed in the provided context.

The study focuses on multiclass classification of seven specific pigmented skin lesion classes, limiting its generalizability to other dermatological conditions or binary classification tasks not included in the study.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized a combined dataset of 14,000 dermoscopy images from three public sources: HAM10000, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020. These sources were pooled together to form a single mixed dataset. Data augmentation was applied to balance the class distribution, expanding the dataset to 28,000 images (4,000 per class). The model was trained and evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation on this combined augmented dataset. No external or unseen test dataset was used for final evaluation.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on a single pooled dataset formed by combining HAM10000, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 images. There is no experiment where the model is trained on one named dataset and tested on a different, unseen named dataset.

training.counts: Part of the 14,000 initial combined images (specific count not isolated in text, but part of the pool)
training.notes: Combined with ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 to form the training pool. Used for model fitting via 10-fold CV.

training.counts: Part of the 14,000 initial combined images
training.notes: Combined with HAM10000 and ISIC-2020. Used for model fitting via 10-fold CV.

training.counts: Part of the 14,000 initial combined images
training.notes: Combined with HAM10000 and ISIC-2019. Used for model fitting via 10-fold CV.

validation.counts: Part of the 14,000 initial images (held out in each CV fold)
validation.notes: Used for model selection/tuning within the 10-fold cross-validation scheme on the pooled dataset.

testing.counts: Part of the 14,000 initial images (held out in each CV fold)
testing.notes: Used for internal testing/evaluation within the 10-fold cross-validation scheme. Results are averaged over the folds.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.dataset: ImageNet (implied by 'pretrained' and standard ShuffleNet usage, though not explicitly named as the source of weights in the provided text snippets, the model is described as 'pretrained Convolution Neural Network' and 'ShuffleNet architecture' which is typically pretrained on ImageNet)
pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The model Light-Dermo is based on the ShuffleNet architecture with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks. The title and abstract refer to it as a 'Pretrained Convolution Neural Network'. Standard ShuffleNet models are pretrained on ImageNet. The text states 'transfer learning (TL)-based CNN model'.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, brightness/contrast adjustment, Gaussian noise, zoom, shear) were applied to the 14,000 original images to create a balanced dataset of 28,000 images (4,000 per class) to address class imbalance.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 10-fold cross-validation on the combined dataset of 14,000 original images (expanded to 28,000 via augmentation). The paper does not specify if the split was patient-level or image-level, but given the pooling of datasets, image-level splitting is implied unless patient IDs were used to ensure separation, which is not explicitly stated as a separation strategy for the CV folds.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 10-fold cross-validation. The folds stayed within the single pooled dataset (HAM10000 + ISIC-2019 + ISIC-2020).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The study acknowledges the lack of external validation in the limitations.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are based on 10-fold cross-validation on the augmented dataset. The model outperformed comparative models like AlexNet (88.9%), MobileNet (90.8%), ResNet18 (89.3%), etc.

generalizability_notes: The study acknowledges limitations regarding high interclass similarity and intraclass variability.
The paper explicitly states that external validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets is not reported.
The dataset was pooled from three sources (HAM10000, ISIC-2019, ISIC-2020) and randomly split via 10-fold CV, which does not constitute true external validation.
Data augmentation was used to balance classes, which may not reflect real-world class prevalence.
No patient-level separation is explicitly confirmed for the cross-validation folds, raising potential leakage risks if images from the same patient appear in both train and test folds.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images contributed by ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 individually (only total 14,000 is given for the combined set).
Whether the 10-fold cross-validation splits were patient-level or image-level.
Specific ImageNet weights used for pretraining (though implied).
Performance on any truly external/unseen dataset.
